coredata.dc:title: Flexible electrode based on nitrogen carbon quantum dots for dopamine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Paulo-Mirasol, Sofia
Izquierdo, Claudia
Alemán, Carlos
Armelin, Elaine
Torras, Juan
coredata.dc:description: The simple fabrication of a flexible electrochemical sensor from nitrogen carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) on a modified ITO surface for dopamine detection is presented. NCQD dispersion was used to modify an ITO-coated PET flexible substrate, which was previously pretreated with oxygen plasma to activate its surface. Optical, chemical, electrochemical and electrocatalytic characterizations were performed on the proposed electrode with UV–vis, FT-IR, TEM, CV, and EIS. The detection limit of the NCQDs-ITO/PET sensor determined via CV was 4.7 µM over a very wide range of 0.0–2.0 mM in a linear regimen. This sensor demonstrated outstanding as a flexible platform and a facile fabrication for the detection of DA without the need to work with composites or complex systems that involve different nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCQDs
Electrochemical sensor
Flexible substrate
Plasma
Dopamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of oxygen-plasma treatment on ITO-PET

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Secretaría de Estado de Investigacion, Desarrollo e Innovacion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This publication is part of the I + D + i project PID2021-125767OB-I00 funded by MCIN/AEI/ 10.13039/501100011033 and by “ERDF A way of making Europe”, by the European Union

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of NCQDs. b) The photoluminescence spectra of NCQDs at different light excitation wavelengths (λexc). Inset: Photograph of the NCQDs dispersion under UV-light lamp (365 nm). c) FTIR spectrum of NCQDs.

(a) TEM image of NCQDs in solution. (b) Histogram showing the particle-size distribution. (c) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of bare ITO-PET electrode (left) and NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode (right).
A wide scan XPS spectrum of (a) NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode. (b-d) High resolution XPS on the NCQDs-ITO/PET spectra of: (b) N 1s; (c) C 1s; and (d) O 1s.

XPS spectra of: bare ITO-PET (green line); oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET (blue line); and NCQDs/ITO-PET (orange line). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Measured EIS spectra for oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET (blue squares) and NCQDs/ITO-PET (orange dots) electrodes: (a) Impedance Bode plot; (b) phase Bode plot; and (c) Nyquist plot; (d) Electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) model applied to fit the experimental impedance output (continuous line curves). The Rs is the electrolyte resistance, CPE1 and Rct are constant phase element related to the ion double layer and resistance, respectively, and CPE2 is the constant phase related to the non-ideal capacitance of ITO nanometric layer. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Cyclic voltammograms for (a) bare ITO-PET and (b) NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes. Voltammograms were obtained at the scan rate of 0.10 V s−1 using 0.1 M LiClO4 aqueous solutions with different DA concentrations.

Calibration curve for DA concentrations ranging from 0.00 up to 2.00 mM in an aqueous solution of 0.5 M LiClO4 using the NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode. The fitting current intensity against DA concentration at (a) 0.6 V and (b) 0.0 V versus Ag|AgCl reference electrode is shown.

Resistance (R) and constant phase elements (CPE) analyzed in 0.5 M KCl solution, from fitting parameters obtained with EEC displayed in Fig. 5d. The error associated to each element of the EEC is written in parentheses.
Comparison of the analytical parameters obtained using different techniques and modified electrodes QDs-based for the determination of DA.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: GCE: glassy carbon electrode, Au NPs: gold nanoparticles, GQD: graphene quantum dot, PL: photoluminescence; SWV: square wave voltammetry; DPV: differential pulse voltammetry.

head.ce:title: Flexible electrode based on nitrogen carbon quantum dots for dopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departament d’Enginyeria Química, EEBE, Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya, C/Eduard Maristany, 10-14, Ed. I.2, Barcelona 08019, Spain
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departament d’Enginyeria Química, EEBE, Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya, C/Eduard Maristany, 10-14, Ed. I.2, Barcelona 08019, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Barcelona Research Center for Multiscale Science and Engineering, Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya, C/Eduard Maristany, 10-14, Ed. I.S, Barcelona 08019, Spain

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Barcelona Research Center for Multiscale Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Barcelona Research Center for Multiscale Science and Engineering, Universitat Politècnica de Catalunya, C/Eduard Maristany, 10-14, Ed. I.S, Barcelona 08019, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The simple fabrication of a flexible electrochemical sensor from nitrogen carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) on a modified ITO surface for dopamine detection is presented. NCQD dispersion was used to modify an ITO-coated PET flexible substrate, which was previously pretreated with oxygen plasma to activate its surface. Optical, chemical, electrochemical and electrocatalytic characterizations were performed on the proposed electrode with UV–vis, FT-IR, TEM, CV, and EIS. The detection limit of the NCQDs-ITO/PET sensor determined via CV was 4.7 µM over a very wide range of 0.0–2.0 mM in a linear regimen. This sensor demonstrated outstanding as a flexible platform and a facile fabrication for the detection of DA without the need to work with composites or complex systems that involve different nanoparticles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Non-toxic metal-free carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained attention as new green nanomaterial for their optical properties, surface functionalization capacity, solubility in solvents other than chloro-solvents, biocompatibility, good conductivity and high photostability [1]. Thanks to these properties, CQDs started to be employed in a broad range of applications, such as bioimaging [2], sensors [3], catalysis[4] and drug delivery [5]. The fluorescence properties of CQDs allow them to be used as optical sensors to detect and quantify molecules, metals, (bio)analytes etc [6–9]. For example, Papadopoulo and coworkers detected nanomaterials, such as zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles (NPs), using a paper sensor based on the fluorescence changes of carbon nanodots [8]. The rational was to detect and quantify the quenching emission intensity when the optical sensor contacted directly with the interested NPs. Also, (bio)analytes are frequent targets of optical carbon and non-carbon quantum dots (QDs)-based sensors [7,9,10]. As a representative case, Mao et al. reported the dopamine (DA) detection by means of carboxy-X-rhodamine (ROX)-DNA-functionalized CdZnTeS QDs [9]. In that study, DA was oxidized to dopaquinone (DQ) under tyrosinase catalysis by dissolved O2, quenching the fluorescence of the CdZnTeS QDs. However, other sensors based in functionalized NPs are exploiting the chemical affinity [10,11] and electrochemical properties [12,13] of QDs to detect bioanalytes.

On the other hand, CQDs can be also used as active material for electrochemical sensors since they exhibit a huge surface/mass ratio and good electrical conductivity [14]. Within this context, it should be mentioned that electrochemical sensors have attracted considerable attention compared to optical sensors due to their intrinsic advantages (e.g., easy to use, rapid response, low-cost, and inexpensive instrumentation) [15].

Neurotransmitters are endogenous molecules that transmit signals between neurons through synapses. Alterations in the levels of specific neurotransmitters are associated with dysfunctions and neurological disorders [16]. Therefore, the analysis and detection of neurotransmitters is of great clinical and pharmaceutical importance, which is reflected in the interest for developing quicker, economic and sensitive analytical methods [17–19]. DA is a well-known neurotransmitter that regulates many biological processes in human body and its maladjustment can divert into a variety of ailments [20]. Thus, high DA levels could lead to cardiovascular diseases such heart attacks and hypertension [21], whilst low DA levels are implicated on several neurological diseases such as Parkinson’s disease, schizophrenia, Alzheimer’s disease, stress and depression [22]. Although the most conventional analytical techniques to quantify the level of DA are mass spectrometry, HPLC, and enzyme assay, which are time consuming and expensive, electrochemical measurements have attracted increasing interest due to their high speed, sensitivity and selectivity [20,23–25]. Indeed, DA biosensors based on electrochemical measurements present important advantages with respect to biosensors based on other analytical techniques, such as small size, portability, fast measurement and low sample size.

Recently, several papers have been reported for DA detection via electrochemical measurements using modified CQDs. Devi et al. [26] studied the electrochemical response to DA of glassy carbon electrodes (GCEs) that were modified by dispersing CQDs on their surface. The utilization of CQDs, which were prepared by means of electrochemical graphite exfoliation, resulted in a calibration curve with a linear regime from 1 to 7 μM of DA and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.099 μM, as measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV). Similarly, Wei et al. [27] modified GCEs using a combination of CQDs and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) for detecting DA together with other biological compounds by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Individual DA detection led to a linear calibration curve from 1 up to 10 μM of DA and a LOD of 0.017 μM.

More recently, several studies reported the utilization of CQDs combined with metal-based compounds were used to quantify the DA level using electrochemical sensors. More specifically, Hasheena et al. [28] used GCEs modified with SnS-CQDs for obtaining a linear calibration curve from 10 up to 500 μM of DA with a LOD of 0.030 μM. Elugoke et al. [29] deposited CQDs mixed with CuO NPs on the surface of GCEs by drop casting. In this case, the LOD, which was determined by square wave voltammetry (SWV), was of 25.4 μM within a linear range of 1–180 μM of DA.

In this work, we synthesized nitrogen doped Carbon Quantum Dots (NCQDs) as an active material to detect and quantify DA in solution. Results show that NCQDs are an efficient way to improve the optoelectronic properties of CQDs. Incorporation of nitrogen at the surface of CQDs alters their chemical properties, enhancing of electrocatalytic activity and tuning of photoluminescence emission wavelength. Furthermore, the added surface functionality is expected to facilitate the interaction with other molecules [30]. NCQDs were deposited on top of indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated polyethylene terephthalate (PET) substrate. The uses of ITO thin film as electrode have been extensively reported either as a support or in a modified form to electrochemically detect DA, among other biomolecules [31,32]. ITO, which is a well-known inorganic semiconductor material employed in many fields because of its high electrical conductivity and optical transparency, becomes flexible when coated with PET. Flexible and transparent materials, such as NCQDs/ITO-PET, have a huge potential to be used in wearable sensors, since they can be adapted to many surfaces such as skin, urine bags etc. [33]. In addition, the use of NCQDs as active material results in very promising sensors due to their outstanding surface area and good electrical conductivity.

All materials and chemicals were used as received and without further purification: Acetone (99.9%), acetonitrile (ACN, 100%), citric acid (99%), dopamine hydrochloride (100%), ethyl acetate (99%), N,N’-dimethylformamide (DMF 99.99%), and urea (98%) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Milli-Q water grade (0.055 S/cm) was used in all synthetic processes.

The NCQDs synthesis was carried out using a bottom up approach via one-step hydrothermal method reported by Chen et al. [34]. Briefly, 1.051 g of citric acid and 2 g of urea were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF. The solution was transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 16 h. The crude of reaction was centrifuged and washed at 4500 rpm with a mixture of ether/ethyl acetate (4:1 v/v). Finally, NCQDs were dispersed in ACN.

ITO-PET samples with a resistivity of 60 Ω cm−1 were firstly cleaned in ultrasonic bath of Milli-Q water, rinsed with acetone and, finally, with isopropanol for 10 min. Then, ITO-PET substrates were treated by plasma treatment under flow of pure oxygen during 2 min, under 0.6 mbar of pressure and 33% of power in order to clean the surface and improve the wettability. Oxygen plasma treatment was carried out with low pressure radio-frequency (RF) plasma (80 MHz), by using a LFG generator 1000 W (Diener Electronic GmbH Co., Germany) and a chamber of 25 dm3. After plasma treatment, a filtered solution of 35 mg mL−1 in ACN of NCQDs was deposited on the substrate surface (i.e. ITO side of the ITO-PET electrode) and, subsequently, spin coated at 1000 rpm during 1 min. In order to increase the thickness of the whole active layer, 10 different layers were consecutively deposited stacked, on top of each other, to obtain the flexible NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode.

UV–vis absorbance spectra were obtained using a Cary100 UV–vis spectrophotometer whilst the photoluminescence measurements of solutions were carried out in a Fluorog Horiba Jobin Ivon spectrophotometer using a 1 cm path length quartz cell.

FT-IR transmittance spectra were recorded on a FTIR Jasco 4100 spectrophotometer. Samples were deposited on an attenuated total reflection accessory (top-plate) with a diamond crystal (Specac model MKII Golden Gate Heated Single Reflection Diamond ATR).

TEM micrographs were recorded using a JEOL JEM J2100 microscope, equipped with a LaB6 thermionic electron gun and operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Images were obtained using a Gatan Orius CCD camera.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed in a SPECS system equipped with a high-intensity twin-anode X-ray source XR50 of Mg/Al (1253 S4 eV / 1487 eV) operating at 150 W, placed perpendicular to the analyzer axis, and using a Phoibos 150 MCD-9 XP detector. The X-ray spot size was 650 µm. The pass energy was set to 25 and 0.1 eV for the survey and the narrow scans, respectively. Charge compensation was achieved with a combination of electron and argon ion flood guns. The energy and emission current of the electrons were 4 eV and 0.35 mA, respectively. For the argon gun, the energy and the emission current were 0 eV and 0.1 mA, respectively. The spectra were recorded with pass energy of 25 eV in 0.1 eV steps at a pressure below 6·10−9 mbar. These standard conditions of charge compensation resulted in a negative but perfectly uniform static charge. The C 1s peak was used as an internal reference with a binding energy of 284.8 eV. The surface composition was determined using the manufacturer's sensitivity factors.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) studies were performed to obtain topographic images of the electrode surface using silicon TAP 150-G probe (Budget Sensors, Bulgaria) with a frequency of 150 kHz and a force constant of 5 N m−1.

Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) methodologies were conducted with a potentiostat/galvanostat an Autolab PGSTAT302N equipped with the ECD module. Measurements were performed with a three-electrode system, where an Ag|AgCl electrode was used as reference electrode and a stainless steel sheet as counter electrode in an aqueous solution of 0.5 M KCl and 0.1 M LiClO4.

The areal specific capacitance (SC, in mF/cm2) was determined using the following expression [35]:
(1)
SC
i
=
Q
v × Δ V × A
where Q is the charge determined by integrating the oxidative or the reductive parts of the cyclic voltammogram curve, ΔV is the potential window (in V), A is the electrode area (in cm2) and v is the scan rate (0.100 V s−1). In all the experiments, the electrodes exposed area for CV analyses was 1.5 cm2. The electrochemical stability of the samples was determined by means of the loss of electroactivity (LEA, in %) measurement against the number of oxidation − reduction cycles:
(2)
LEA
i
=
Q 2
-
Q i
Q 2
× 100
where Q2 is the oxidation charge (in C) of the second evaluated oxidation − reduction cycle, whilst Qi is the oxidation charge of the i-th oxidation − reduction cycle. The first cycle was discarded due to the structural relaxation undergone by the polymer that forms the film.

The water contact angle (WCA) was determined using the water drop approach by OCA 15EC (DataPhysics Instruments GmbH Filderstadt). The SCA20 software was used to measure the WCA, which is shown in this work as the average of at least 10 measures for each condition.

As-prepared NCQDs were characterized by spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. Fig. 1 a-b shows the UV–vis spectrum for NCQDs in acetonitrile and the photoluminescence spectra of NCQDs at different excitation wavelength. Both measurements were carried out at room temperature. The absorbance spectrum presents two broad bands at 245 nm and 350 nm and two shoulders at 275 and 400 nm. The absorption bands at 245 and 275 nm belongs to π-π* transition band of C = C, while the bands at 350 and 400 nm are attributed to the n-π* transition and C = O/C = N, respectively [36]. The NCQDs photoluminescence spectra were measured at different light excitation wavelengths (λexc). The spectrum obtained at λexc = 460 nm (Fig. 1b) shows an emission wavelength of NCQDs at 530 nm. Typically, NCQDs present excitation dependence fluorescence, which means that there is a red shift of the emission wavelength when the excitation wavelength increased. This phenomenon is due to the different defect states of CQDs surface groups on the surface of the NCQDs and the size distribution [37].

ATR-FTIR measurements of powder NCQDs were carried out at room temperature (Fig. 1c). The FT-IR showed a very broad peak at 3186 cm−1, which belongs to the intermolecular stretching vibration of the O–H bond corresponding to a carboxylic groups holding intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Other characteristic bands are: the strong peak at 1560 cm−1 (stretching of C = O); the peak at 1335 cm−1 (stretching of C–N); the peak at 1116 cm−1 (stretching of C–O); and the small shoulder at 1646 cm−1 (vibration of the N–C = O bond) [38,39].

The morphology (i.e. size and the shape) of the synthesized NCQDs was analyzed in solution by means of TEM images at × 25000 of magnification (Fig. 2 a). NCQDs present a spherical shape with a uniform dispersion in solution. The particle size dispersion histogram (Fig. 2b), presents a narrow Gaussian distribution (n = 20) with a very uniform particle-size average of 1.94 ± 0.21 nm. The surface topography of NCQDs film deposited on the top of ITO-PET was studied by AFM. The AFM images (Fig. 2c) show a good dispersion of NCQDs through the superficial layer with an Rq of 3.66 nm. However, some large surface regions with a diameter of about 100 nm can be observed, suggesting a trend towards aggregation of the NCQDs at the deposition time. On the other hand, the image of the bare ITO-PET electrode shows no particles on the surface, and a much smoother RMS roughness (2.47 nm) compared with NCQDs film.

Elemental and surface chemical properties of NCQDs were studied by means of XPS measurements. Fig. 3 a shows the XPS survey spectrum that revealed three main peaks with intense signals at 531.22 eV (O 1s), 284.62 (C 1s) and 399.22 eV (N 1s), representing an atomic concentration on the elementary analysis of 53.7%, 30.0% and 10.0% of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen elements, respectively. In Fig. 3b, the high-resolution peak of the N 1s spectrum at 399.22 eV has been attributed to the amine (−NH − ) bond [40]. In addition, the spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 3c) of NCQDs is deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.62 eV, 286.90 eV and 288.11 eV which have been assigned to C−C(sp2), (sp2)C–N and C=O bonds [41,42]. Finally, high-resolution peak of O 1s spectrum at 531.22 eV is attributed to C=O bond (to super-imposed peak of the O–H, C=O and O–O bonds) [43,44]. The rest of the peaks are ascribed to oxygen contamination of ITO-PET surface due to the exposure of the samples to air before the XPS. The above discussion confirmed the presence of nitrogen and oxygen besides carbon in NCQDs.

Fig. 4 compares the XPS spectra recorded for oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET and NCQDs/ITO-PET. The XPS spectrum of the bare oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET presents three major peaks of O 1s, C 1s and N 1s, besides in peaks, with atomic concentration of 40.1%, 37.1%, and 0.9%, respectively. However, the XPS spectrum of NCQDs/ITO-PET presents an increase on the C1s and N1s compositions with respect to the oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET surface (ΔC 1s = 16.6% and ΔN 1s = 9.1%). The main peak contributions are due to the –NH- and C=O bonds (Fig. 3). Thus, confirming that NCQDs was successfully deposited on the top of ITO-PET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of oxygen-plasma treatment on ITO-PET
ce:section.ce:para: The ITO-PET surface was exposed to oxygen plasma treatment before depositing the NCQDs to clean and remove any particle adsorbed on the film. Plasma treatment is one of the most used surface methods to increase adhesion strength between substrate and coating by improving the wettability [45]. Figure S1 shows the cyclic voltammograms of ITO-PET electrodes before and after oxygen plasma treatment, which were recorded using a potential window of − 0.5 V and 1.0 V vs Ag|AgCl and a scan rate of 0.10 V s−1. As it can be seen, the current density and the voltammogram area were significantly enhanced when the ITO-PET surface was treated. Indeed, the specific capacitance (Eq (1)) increased from 4.6 ± 0.6 μF cm−2 to 5.1 ± 0.2 μF cm−2 after surface treatment.

The WCA was determined (n > 10) to observe the effect of plasma treatment in the surface wettability (Figure S2). The average WCA value for the untreated ITO-PET surface was of 82° ± 2, indicating poor wettability, whilst a reduction to 37° ± 8 was obtained after the oxygen-plasma treatment. This important improvement in the wettability of the ITO-PET surface is due to the incorporation of new surface functional groups. Overall, oxygen plasma causes important changes on the surface of ITO-PET, improving the interface with NCQDs. This change is expected to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity of the NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode against the detection of DA [46].

The electroactivity of oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET (control) and NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes were characterized by CV and EIS using a three-electrode system, where Ag|AgCl was used as reference electrode and steel as counter electrode in a 0.1 M LiClO4 solution in Mili-Q. All the tests were done at room temperature.

CV measurements were performed by a scanning a potential window ranging from −0.5 to 1.0 V vs Ag|AgCl at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. The specific capacitance of oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET and NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes, which was obtained using the second redox cycle, was 5.1 ± 0.2 μF cm−2 and 8.1 ± 1.1 μF cm−2, respectively. These values confirm the improvement of the sensor after depositing the NCQDs on the treated ITO-PET surface. However, comparison of the voltammograms recorded for oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET and NCQDs/ITO-PET during 30 consecutive redox cycles reflected the electrochemical instability of the NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode with respect to the control one (Figure S3). In fact, the electrochemical activity of treated ITO-PET and NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes experienced a reduction of 17.0% and 67.1%, respectively, after 30 cycles. The highest LEA observed for NCQDs/ITO-PET could be partially attributed to the desorption and, consequently, loss of part of the NCQDs deposited on the ITO-PET surface.

EIS measurements were carried out to study the changes in the resistance and capacitance of the sample after the oxygen-plasma treatment and NCQDs deposition. EIS is a non-destructive technique to study the interfacial layer between the sample and the working electrode [47,48]. The impedance was measured between 10−1 Hz and 105 Hz in an aqueous solution of 0.5 M KCl. Fig. 5 shows the results for the oxygen-plasma treated ITO-PET (control) and NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes.

The impedance Bode plot (Fig. 5a) clearly differentiate two time constants (σ1 and σ2), one associated to the material interface and ITO layer (NCQDs particles deposited and polar groups from plasma treatment), at high frequencies; and the second associated to the dielectric surface of the ITO oxide layer in contact with PET substrate, at low frequencies. The presence of NCQDs particles cause an increase of |Z| values and phase angles with respect to the ITO-PET surface after plasma etching (high frequencies). Fig. 5b shows the phase Bode plot, which is used for the evaluation of the capacitive systems. In the Bode plot, the phase angles are low (∼18-28°) at high frequencies, as expected for conducting materials, and increases to 80° at high frequencies, due to the ITO layer, that actuates as a dielectric barrier. [49].

Fig. 5c shows the Nyquist plot of the two studied systems where the evolution of the imaginary part of the impedance (−Z'') versus its real part (Z′) was represented. Nyquist plots are very useful to evaluate the electric double layer, electrode/electrolyte resistance and charge transport in the electrode surface [50]. Table 1 lists the contribution of each element of the EEC (Fig. 5d), being the quality on the data fitting evaluated by means of the error percentages associated with each element. Both systems present an electrolyte resistance (Rs) in parallel with a thin porous material resistance (Rct), called charge transfer resistance, and a constant phase element (CPE1), followed by another constant phase element (CPE2) in series (Fig. 5d) [51]. The non-ideal capacitance element, CPE, is usually present as a result of the sample surface defects, such as porosity and surface roughness. However, thin oxide layers such as ITO, can be modeled appropriately with quasi ideal capacitors (CPE2), with an “n” value close to 1 (Table 1), due to its very smooth and homogeneous characteristics [52].

The electrolyte resistance (Rs) is the parameter related with the interaction between the electrolyte and the surface of the electrode. The Rs values found after EEC fitting were 58.9 Ω and 83.10 Ω, for the blank (plasma treated ITO-PET) and NCQDs/ITO-PET, respectively. These values indicate a good charge transfer property between the electrode and the aqueous solution interface [53].

Plasma treated ITO-PET and NCQDs/ITO-PET semicircles corresponding to the Rct and CPE1 elements appear at high frequencies, while a CPE2 element appears at low frequencies. The CPE1, which is attributed to a double-layer interface, depends on the porosity of the material, the thickness, etc. Thus, while the first CPE is related to the double layer of the system, the second one is related to the non-ideal capacitance through the ITO-PET substrate. The capacitive arc corresponds to the effects of the CPE and its diameter is related to the electrolyte resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct). It is observed that the arc radius of the impedance curves (Fig. 5c) is smaller for the oxygen-plasma ITO-PET treated electrode than for the NCQDs/ITO-PET one, indicating the low impedance of ITO-PET compared with NCQDs/ITO-PET [50]. This difference is due to the large addition of NCQDs on ITO-PET electrode (i.e. ITO surface), slightly restricting the charge diffusion between electrode and electrolyte [49].

These results indicate that the layer of NCQDs was successfully deposited on the ITO-PET electrode and confirm the high capacitance of NCQDs particles. Data from EIS spectra fitted to the EEC are summarized in Table 1. The chi-square of the EEC fitting was in the order of 10-3-10-4, corroborating the good adjustment to experimental results.

Eddin et al. [54] presented a study about the interaction between carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and dopamine by Refractive Index Surface Plasmon Resonance. The surface characterization of CQDs was performed before and after the dopamine addition confirming the alteration groups of the CQDs surface due to DA bond on the surface. In their FTIR of CQDs after the exposure to the DA, the intensity of the CQDs’ peak of the –OH stretching decreased, the peak of C–H stretching vibrations was red shifted and increased its intensity. From the FTIR analysis, they conclude that DA was anchored onto the CQDs surface, which can be extrapolated to our system.

To test the electroactivity of NCQDs, DA detection assays were conducted by CV using the prepared NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode. Fig. 6 shows the voltammograms for bare ITO-PET and NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes in solutions with different DA concentrations, which were recorded between −0.5 V and 1.0 V at the scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. In presence of DA, the voltammograms present an anodic peak potential, (Ep)a, at 0.68 V, and a cathodic peak potential, (Ep)c, at −0.2 V in presence of DA [55], which corresponds to the reversible oxidation of DA to DQ.

With each addition of DA, the oxidation peak at 0.6 V increased linearly and the reduction peak decreased with respect to DA concentration. This suggests that DA exhibits a quasi-reversible behavior [56]. To better understand, the electrochemical response of NCQDs/ITO-PET against DA, the same measures were performed using bare ITO-PET. Although similar anodic and cathodic peaks were observed at 0.6 V and 0.0 V [57] when bare ITO-PET was compared to NCQDs/ITO-PET (Fig. 6a and Figure S4), a significant enhancement in the peak current density occurred when NCQD was used as active material. This observation confirms the potential of NCQDs/ITO-PET electrodes to detect and quantify bioanalytes, such as DA, in solution.

It is known that pH of the solutions has an influence directly into the protonation of dopamine. This change can interfere with electrode surface interaction. Kim and coworkers [58] showed a study on the effect of the pH and scan rate to the electrooxidation of serotonin and dopamine using a WS2/Graphene/Polyimide electrode. They showed how the peak current varied with changing the pH of the buffer with a maximum of 7.5 in both cases. Similarly, He et al. [59] showed an Amine-Modified Magnetite-Electrochemically Reduced Graphene Oxide Nanocomposite Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode for detecting dopamine in solution. They observed that the intensity potential anodic peak increases in acidic media with the highest value at pH 3.5. In this work, LiClO4 solution at neutral pH was used to detect and quantify dopamine.

The performance of the new NCQDs/ITO-PET flexible electrode to detect DA was examined by estimating analytical parameters through calibration plots. For this purpose, the detection of DA was quantified using three different NCQDs/ITO-PET samples, which were prepared under the same experimental conditions. Fig. 7 shows the calibration curve of the sensor, representing the evolution of the peak current density against the concentrations of DA considering both the oxidation of DA to DQ at 0.68 V and the reduction of DQ to DA at (−0.18 V).

The calibration curve shows that the NCQDs/ITO-PET sensor works in one linear plot, with correlation coefficient R2 = 0.998 at 0.68 V and R2 = 0.989 at (−0.18 V) for concentrations ranging from 0.00 mM to 2.00 mM.

The limit of detection (at S/N = 3) obtained in the first regime for the plot displayed in Fig. 7a was as low as 4.7 μM, while the sensitivity was 4.15·10-5 μA mM−1 cm−2. This calibration presents an improved limit of the sensitivity when is compared with bare ITO-PET electrode (Figure S4).

The performance of NCQDs/ITO-PET electrode sensor was compared with other methods based on CQDs for DA detection reported in the literature (Table 2 ). Most of the reported CQDs-based sensors were fabricated by modifying the properties of GCEs, screen printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) or new compounds based on a mixture of different inorganic nanoparticles to detect DA. In this work, ITO patterned in a flexible substrate of PET was used, after an oxygen-plasma surface treatment, by direct deposition of NCQDs by spin coating. The LOD of the NCQDs/ITO-PET sensor against the detection of AD is greater than perhaps some of the CQDs results shown in the bibliography (Table 2). However, the ease of manufacturing of this electrode, as well as the broad extension of the linear range behavior represent an important advantage to measure the concentration of DA in different body fluids/tissues, unlike other CQDs electrodes with a much shorter linear detection range.

The concentration of DA in the human body can range from a few pM to several mM, depending on the body fluid or tissue used for measurement [63,64]. More specifically, the level of this neurotransmitter is ∼ 0.35 nM in plasma, ∼3 µM in human urine, and could reach up to a few mM in some fluids and/or tissues of the central nervous system and in the periphery (such as ∼ 520 µM in microbiome (supernatant), ∼1.1 mM in caudate/putamen and ∼ 2.4 mM in adrenal gland) [63–66]. The broad working range of this electrode together with the significant increase in sensitivity due to the presence of NCQDs on the surface of the ITO-PET, makes this an interesting electrode to be used in the detection of DA or other potential bioanalytes.

Although CV has been used to determine dopamine and other neurotransmitters for many years, selectivity remains an analytical challenge to overcome. In fact, neurotransmitters such as dopamine, epinephrine, norepinephrine and DOPAC present a similar electrochemical behavior [67], as well as other compounds with similar peak potentials, which can cause selectivity problems in this type of technique. Thus, new strategies focused on the fabrication of more selective electrodes and new data analysis strategies are necessary [68].

In this work, we present a flexible electrode based of Nitrogen doped Carbon quantum dots deposited on top of ITO-PET pretreated with oxygen-plasma for DA detection. The NCQDs has been synthetized by hydrothermal method using non-chlorinated solvents and common green materials as citric acid and urea. The electrode has been tested to quantify DA in solution via cyclic voltammetry. The sensor shows a promising application for accurate DA detection in some body fluids, considering its LOD (4.7 μM) and a wide linear range from 0 to 2.0 mM. The NCQDs-ITO/PET electrode was characterized by FTIR, XPS, AFM, CV and EIS confirming its strong ability for electron transfer, contributing to the improvement of the conductivity and as consequence its sensitivity towards DA detection. This work presents a flexible platform for the detection of DA without the need to work with composites or complex systems that involve different nanoparticles.

Sofia Paulo-Mirasol: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Claudia Izquierdo: Data curation. Carlos Alemán: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Elaine Armelin: Validation, Formal analysis. Juan Torras: Supervision, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2023.157241.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Zheng, R. Huang, X. Ma, J. Tang, Z. Li, X. Wang, J. Wei, J. Wang, A Highly Sensitive Dopamine Sensor Based on Graphene Quantum Dots Modified Glassy Carbon Electrode, Int. J. Electrochem. Sci., 13 (2018) 5723-5735.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Anatomical and affinity state comparisons between dopamine D1 and D2 receptors in the rat central nervous system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.K. Richfield, J.B. Penney, A.B. Young, Anatomical and affinity state comparisons between dopamine D1 and D2 receptors in the rat central nervous system, Neuroscience, 30 (1989) 767-777.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Where is dopamine and how do immune cells see it?: Dopamine-mediated immune cell function in health and disease

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.M. Matt, P.J. Gaskill, Where Is Dopamine and how do Immune Cells See it?: Dopamine-Mediated Immune Cell Function in Health and Disease, J. NeuroImmune Pharmacol., 15 (2020) 114-164.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.J. Steckl, P. Ray, Stress Biomarkers in Biological Fluids and Their Point-of-Use Detection, ACS Sens., 3 (2018) 2025-2044.
